["cog","iug","<p>As I&#39;ve been working the last few years on my mapping and visualization ideas, I&#39;ve also read quite a bit of stuff about human cognition.  These two things are related, and it also matches AI efforts.  One effort tries to map meaning and relations, kind of like what I do.  This was what people tried to do in the 1970s and 1980s that failed.  It also failed in the 1860s (seriously) and the 2010s.  Currently there are successful efforts (Barry Smith, NASA, US military, BFO are all related, and arguably successful).  Mostly, these days, what we call AI are  models and algorithms that  parse streams of real-time data.  In other words, AI to many, is fancy reporting.  I&#39;ve seen some hybrid efforts as well.  I&#39;m not going to get into any of these things, though (but I&#39;m sure you know where to look if you want to know more).</p>\n<p>One thing that has puzzled me, is how come, if I can run my experiments, and they match up, and I can validate my models and understanding pretty much universally, why is it that people get so stuck cognitively?  Why is what I&#39;m saying so foreign, when it has been around since the 1800s?  People have fooled me.  I think they are following, but they tip their hand late in the game, and I realize they aren&#39;t tracking at all.  They act like they are.  Most recently, I had 20 solid hours back and forth, mind meld with one of the top data scientists in the area.  Finally, I asked him what it was that I was so excited about with triples.  He failed.  I get it that it is also true that I failed.  I&#39;m working on remedying that.  Ultimately, it is a failure to relate with my audience, regardless of how well my models hold up historically or against current challenges.</p>\n<p>But that isn&#39;t all that puzzles me.  The blue vs. red tendency bothers me.  Well... this entry is going on a bit.  I wanted to share one thing, a gem of a paper on human cognition, that explains human behavior quite well.  I call it &quot;chimp notches&quot; in my journal, and often relate back to it.  The paper is no longer available, so grab the wayback copy <a href=\"https://web.archive.org/web/20070418142809/http://groups.inf.ed.ac.uk/ppig/readinglist/tomasello_et_al2004.pdf\">here</a>.</p>\n<p>The punchline to the article is that our superpower, at one notch above chimps, is that we can gauge shared intention with three tools and two teams. This means that unless we have other shared cognitive aids (maps, reports, etc.), we aren&#39;t that great at gauging an extremely complicated world and many, many teams. We are led to believe we are, as that can be monetized or used for control. We are running at cognitive capacity, as far as gauging shared intention towards goals, utilizing tools. In other words, education using abstract ideas, since the current situation is always unique, as well as shared meaning and knowledge, is the only way to emerge out of crisis with a true goal that serves interests that we hold important as humans. You can see where this is going, and why it fits my <a href=\"#gy1\">statement of purpose</a>, etc. One new thing I&#39;ve realized recently, is that the analysis tools I&#39;m building, why I&#39;m so excited about them, is that they can be understood by humans within the cognitive limits. We need to be able to understand what we are doing and why, <em>as humans</em> cognitively. Flows can be measured, yes (truing up the flying plane, etc.), but why and how, as well as goals cannot, and should not be handled primarily on the streams and flows. Well... running longish again... much more at (you know where).</p>\n<p>The <a href=\"https://web.archive.org/web/20070418142809/http://groups.inf.ed.ac.uk/ppig/readinglist/tomasello_et_al2004.pdf\">article</a> didn&#39;t get into this, but a related idea is how our memories are mapped. We experience in streams, but since storage is mapped, it means we can mis-associate new streams. It isn&#39;t like that is the only way our brain functions, of course, but it does mean that programming/mind control is stronger than we usually accept. Ugh... and here I am posting ... ummm... here, where most already have their maps all laid out. Blue or red. Agile or waterfall. Doer or teacher. Two teams, right? A few tools? I wouldn&#39;t trade where I&#39;m at. I have something I want to do. Still, this is a lifetime of learning right here... way too much for me. <a href=\"https://youtu.be/9zZfBBp-j6I\">I don&#39;t want another life</a>. This one is exhausting, but I wouldn&#39;t trade it. The more I push up against the current crop of humans, as they destroy ecosystems for vertebrates, while I love, and understand, I can&#39;t help but think that it is futile in the present. Most are comfortable. Many have given up on understanding, or push understanding off to a nebulous other-worldly entity (Judeo-Christian, BigSite CloudGlobal, HeroIndustrialist, etc.). In the end, the result is the same. We plod on in our destruction, wimpering about our situation, but unable to pull ourselves up, unwilling to change our maps, too busy in our cush, entitled, entertained, treadmill lives. I have to <a href=\"#cpo\">hope in Gen Z</a>, I guess, and do what I can to push my tiny <a href=\"#v2v\">dreamboat</a> into the stream.</p>\n"],

["y06","iug","<p>I&#39;ve been struggling with anger.  The problem is that I am getting more sleep.  How do those two go together?  When I get more sleep I face the world more completely. One of the messages in this movie is that you can&#39;t really know if the world has a place for you or not.  It is your network of human relations, as well as effects on the world simply due to your human existence that you are unaware of, that form the value of your life. TikTok recently revived the story, too.) I do know to my bones that our conscious mind is just the tip of the iceberg (nod to Freud who used the exact metaphor in his book Ego and Id). ["oe7","b88","Complete Ideas"],
["oe7","ydm","2022-10-16"],
["oe7","iug","<p>One of the more unique principles I&#39;ve found, as I blindly trace the veins in the rock I&#39;m transforming, is that a complete idea just needs one version.  What I&#39;m working with, the world of operating systems, JSON, scripts, triples, and HTML, currently thrives in a different world.  It lives in the constant-change world, where ideas are fluid based on product/user feedback and corporate interests.  In the constant-change world, a complete idea is assumed to not be immediately relevant due to the fluid nature.  And, of course, the world is in constant change; that is obvious.  At the same time, though, a well-formed idea, a complete design, is useful, and it does not require constant change.  </p>\n<p>Historically, a complete idea has been expressed with writing, mostly as books.  This is how frameworks of connected ideas come together.  This isn&#39;t the only expression.  Public living spaces can be a completely expressed idea, particularly if they are trimmed to core needs or observation.  Stonehenge might be considered an expressed idea, or perhaps an amphitheater.  Some kinds of art might be complete ideas - most, I think - but the flexibility of writing generates the majority of complete ideas.</p>\n<p>I am focused on common meaning as I write this, so this introduces some constraints.  A piece of art might be a complete idea for the artist, but the meaning will change by viewer.  An amphitheater as an architectural form has meaning that is fairly static, but arguably the people change the meaning over time.  Sometimes there are lions in the basement.  (Lions in the Basement... rock band name).  Writing itself has constraints.  Words mean different things to different people, and culture changes broader understanding.  But, we are now getting into ontology or even epistemology, which, while I track the ontology part, is not my main point here.  I will add that since the 1960s, we have assumed that we would need computers to express complete ideas within complex systems.</p>\n<p>The idea that we need computers for complete meaning, ironically, is where I&#39;m diverging.  Who is my audience for a complete idea?  I&#39;m writing for humans.  Humans don&#39;t change cognitively.  Sure, they get more distracted.  Sure, their cognitive maps vary in complexity and depth; however, we have all been cognitively stable for thousands of years as far as hardware.  An idea, expressed completely, at least for a useful topic that is broader than the profit of a new kind of oatmeal, should be able to stand over time.  If I tie the expression of that idea into a complex, constantly changing storage, compute, and presentation platform, I lose both agency and persistence.  This is where extreme consideration of the mechanisms for any expression of an idea is critical.  A 500 page series of words (book) fits many needs, but it fails in flexibility.  So, if I am expressing an idea completely, particularly if the topic is embedded in information technology developed over the last 60 years, it is very difficult to do if I&#39;m tending to the issues of agency and persistence.</p>\n<p>This is the stance I&#39;ve been working from for many years, starting with nonic in 2006.  I want to stress, though, that it is the idea that requires the completeness, disconnected from the constant change.  This doesn&#39;t mean that the constant change is irrelevant.  Take any great book that expresses an idea.  Take a great design.  These are independent from the week-to-week flow of product/users/interests/politics.  They can and should exist outside of a handful of overlords.  We can and should be able to create complete ideas ourselves.  We should be able to read them as well.  We should not cede that ability for a lead role in a cage (to borrow from Pink Floyd).  Yes, there is and will be constant change, and the velocity will accelerate.  Does the fact that a completely expressed idea mean that it has to be <em>the one</em>?  No.   Although, my experience is that most people prefer to just kick the can down the road one week at a time.  </p>\n<p>OK.  Bring this down to the specific, mister.  When I started my ontology project in May, 2019, the tools available were not good enough.  Still, even yesterday, I was on one of my final demos, a single page app that combined 576 OS build steps, hashes, and compile outputs into one 17 MB HTML presentation, and I hit a tech block.  The Javascript compression routine I found I liked best can&#39;t deal with some unicode sequences.  This is particularly weird, because I&#39;m using an API call that sounds like it should.  I have to wrap my own unicode routine inside the other one.   There is a recursive nature to this paragraph.  I&#39;m posting from the custom OS I developed to deal with all of the weirdness.  And, while the issues with unicode have mostly been resolved in the latest OS distributions as of today, the only way I know to do all of the things I need to do with certainty, is with my own OS, and this single page is an expression of what that means.  We have arrived at the point of the post: I only need to do that once.</p>\n<p>It turns out I had 10 demo sites, different schemas and knowledge areas.  These are needed to show how the expressed idea, the design for triples system analysis, works, to concretize the complete idea.  And, when I&#39;m done, I should be <em>done</em>.  Yes, there will be follow-on ideas, but expressing this design is something I can do just once.  That includes my OS.  It is a PITA to keep it maintained.  The core idea is much, much more important.</p>\n<p>I don&#39;t have a monopoly on this.  I&#39;m sure everybody has expressed complete ideas.  I know some of you have written and published books.  What is likely unique with my viewpoint is  that it is embedded in the domain of IT, in which most assume useful expression is not static, and even when it is, it isn&#39;t useful against the very dynamic web of change it lives within.</p>\n"],

Regardless, what I saw is important.  It is important.</p>\n<p>I can see, looking back, how my approach has been ill-advised and futile.  For one, calling attention to the fact that our lack at establishing individual systemic agency is a form of looting, is ill-advised.  I also have some hard-wired difficulties.  That is not an excuse for bad (or ill-advised) behavior, but it is also not something I can eliminate.  I can temper, try again, etc., but I will always be a little off.  I will say that one ill-advised behavior is also a plus.  I rarely accept &quot;because that is the way it is&quot; as an answer.  As for the looting, a large part of this is my emotional response to watching my civilization destroy the ecosystems we depend on.  It is looting by definition.  It is probably ill-advised to kick off a conversation with that, but it is understandable.</p>\n<p>What I&#39;m chasing, presenting, developing is not new.  It fits less and less with the vector of cognitive motion in our society, particularly with the active rewiring and stream stacking, but it is critically important, and I&#39;m not backing down from this.   To make it productive, though, I have to get at the root of why.  My Taos goal was communication, so ultimately I&#39;m not presenting new knowledge, just communicating existing knowledge, as well as synthesizing it with my experience in system analysis.  One thing that is recent, is that I know that long word form (technical solution descriptions that hit anything over 10 pages) is not the way to rewire system cognition for readers.  It is a way to bootstrap a new form of system cognition, though, since it currently exists (people send rockets to the moon based on that form of mapping system knowledge).</p>\n<p>If you missed a key part of my focus, perhaps because it was obscured by &quot;let&#39;s fight now&quot; wording (looter), my focus is on human cognition, and not machine cognition.  We started a focus on using computers as a crutch with direct cognition vs. using computers to help us rewire our cognition maps intentionally, starting in the 1960s.  Why is it important to focus on human cognition, you ask?  Because everything we do collaboratively with shared intention is related.  At the root of all of this are the ideas of Michael Tomasello. </p>\n<p> <a href=\"https://web.archive.org/web/20211028021720/https://philpapers.org/archive/GONMTB-2.pdf\">Here is Tomasello&#39;s PDF</a></p>\n"],

["adi","b88","Word Salad"],
["adi","ydm","2022-10-28"],
["adi","iug","<p>Words are great for flowing streams of memories and stories, but they are limited in dimension for most readers.  Technical documents use words and figures, and attempt to address the gap of systems understanding.  Law and medicine also have steep curves and dense words.  The knowledge artifacts of these disciplines take education and resources that are not available to most, so we rely on third parties: doctors, lawyers, engineers, architects, and cloud.</p>\n<p>We are at cognitive capacity, yet we are buffeted by streams thrown against ontological ships owned be others.  What is the meaning of <em>our</em> voyage?  Where do <em>we</em> want to go?  Most want similar things, yet the cognitive limits of humans require us to fall back to mere streams and two dimensional analysis.  We are aware of the perilous voyage, but have no agency.  The disconnect between the ontological ship and agency is exploited for profit.</p>\n<p>How do we start collaboratively mapping our voyage, and in a direction of our choosing, with outcomes of our choosing, when the systems are so complicated that detailed description becomes a word salad?  The answer is in front of you, those reading this.  The entire world-wide-web is a giant graph.  DNS is a tree, with CNAMEs between the leafs.  A website is a leaf with pages for trees.  Zoom into the page as leaf, and within those leaves are head, body, and pieces of the body (divs).  Within the divs are other components with attributes.  And, every single one of these has a simple underlying concept, the triple (subject - predicate - object).  It seems too simple, but not if you realize we are mostly at two, not three. &quot;Two&quot; is a key-value pair against a time series: At 5:00 we had a temperature of 30 degrees.  At 5:10 we had a temperature of 33 degrees: 5:00 - 30, 5:10 - 33.  </p>\n<p>What does a triple give us?  It gives us a way to collaboratively establish meaning.  It gives us a way to agree on a destination.  How?  A triple is a solid atom of knowledge that can be refined to agreement and built out with many different &quot;agencies&quot; working together.   We - on - ship, ship - made_of - wood, wood - needs - finish, varnish - kind_of - finish, etc.  Or, in WWW terms, website - title - SuperWebSite, website - favelink1 - url, favelink1 - has_image sun, etc.  Triples can be established in the middle and grow like grass, a  rhizome, without the need for a pyramid peak or base*.  This is why the web expanded so quickly.  Agency can begin at zero dot zero dot zero, origin.  You (and all of us) can start at origin and build meaning, reclaim agency, and even have our own origins.  We can set the course of our ship correctly at any time, no matter when we come to our senses.  We can rebuild our ship, own our ontology ourselves, force it to our needs as they change.</p>\n<p>It is possible.  It is useful to pursue. Don&#39;t just take the cruise without agency.</p>\n<p>My two cents anyway.  Forgive the word salad.</p>\n"],

["pts","b88","The Flight of the Phoenix"],
["pts","ydm","2023-01-09"],
["pts","iug","<p>For years I have wondered why, as humans within complex systems, we have less and less of a grasp on our own telemetry, both individually, and as a species.  The prevailing idea is that the systems are simply too complicated to understand, so we need to rely on computers, cloud, and hero industrialists to solve our problems for us.  We are relegated to being black-box consumers.  The problem is that this becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy and trap. As we rely more and more on black-box cognition, focused on tweaking streams of metrics to true up the plane, rather than understanding what keeps it in the air, we lose the skills to establish holistic telemetry within systems.  Use it or lose it.  If you lose it, somebody else will gladly sell you a service.  But, buyer beware, without agency, the goals of the black-box cognition provider become your goals. </p>\n<p>We have a responsibility as individuals and as a species to cognitively understand our telemetry within complex systems.  This is required for us to make informed choices, to make sense of the world we live in.  The challenge is: how do we reclaim cognitive agency and engage responsively with the massively complex systems we have created in our civilization, without ceding cognition to the black-box vendors? We will need hero industrialists. We will need cloud.  We will need machine learning and AI.  But we also need to have individual agency in the cognitive exoskeletons we use to collaboratively establish telemetry, requirements, goals, and, ultimately, progress towards a shared, intentional future. Frankly, this is a huge challenge, one that I am attempting to address, but as I watch, the world is shrinking from individual and organizational telemetry and cognition and working as fast as they can to outsource it to black-box services.</p>\n<p>Whether or not we can actually do this, whether we have enough runway to face the hard realities and participate as individuals and a species in our future with shared cognition before broad collapse, is not my concern.  If we have no more runway, we will simply be... well... at the end of the runway in the grass.  But, eventually, we will need to regauge telemetry within the remaining systems.  Eventually we will need to work collaboratively.  When the plane crashes, the IoT feed stops, no?  As far as individual action, it can only help to hone these skills and tools.  Anybody see the movie The Flight of the Phoenix?  It is one of my favorites. It fits well into my metaphors here.</p>\n"],

["tb3","b88","Rowboats not robots!"],
["tb3","ydm","2023-01-10"],
["tb3","iug","<p>Rowboats not robots!</p>\n<p>Perhaps I&#39;m wrong.  I&#39;m sure I&#39;m wrong about many things.  But, I did participate in the growth of computers, starting with acoustically coupled modems on the top of dumb terminals.  I&#39;ve carted in old 386 hardware with Slackware GNU/Linux installed to replace the unstable NT DNS with BIND at work, where it managed name resolution for a nationwide consulting firm.  I did DevOps in 1996 by having acquisitions fill out a spreadsheet and generating file and identity/authentication servers automatically from the spreadsheet (filled out originally by non-technical people in Excel, mind you, not a proprietary JSON/YAML-ish format for the CI/CD of the year).</p>\n<p>I know the pain of dependencies for an operating system, as I&#39;ve built them from scratch, down to the GNU C Library (I&#39;m posting this from an os with my own compile of glibc-2.30 at the bottom of the stack). I&#39;ve used all of these.  I&#39;ve watched as folks with thin experience discovered source control and turned it into a religion.  But I think we lost our way a bit at sea.  We lost our ability as humans to clearly describe where we are, where we want to be, and how to get there.  We ceded that ability to third parties that sold us black box everything as a service  BBEaaS.</p>\n<p>As I&#39;ve worked my way through implementing, operating, designing, and analyzing business processes and information systems through the years, I have also worked with web technologies.  The standards in place that allow BBEaaS to operate profitably without needing too much compute, and mitigate latency issues, these standards can provide local-first analysis and visualization platforms, a rowboat for human cognition, rather than robots that provide yet another BBEaaS. I&#39;m talking about true things outside of domain.</p>\n<p>&quot;Domain&quot; is a graph analysis term.  I&#39;ve stumbled into the domain of where the word &quot;domain&quot; is used.  And this is outside of the archetypes and Jung world.  We all work within domains of understanding.  While it is possible to break out - to go in to a different domain, at the level of complexity we live at, with the fractured surface experience - we have almost infinite domains of experience to share.  Streams cut through that, and the less real information that is presented, the more likely we find something to latch onto as we observe from our domains.</p>\n<p>We have leveraged machines to help us, yet that does nothing to help us as humans, to discuss what is true.  It isn&#39;t merely the intangibles like the musty forest experience, it is the real things cross domain that cannot be named.  How is it possible to name things cross domain at a level that is humanly understandable: real communication and cognition?  And, as we rely on streams and computers for the assist in our exponentially complicated, nested systems, we lose the cross-domain cognitive capabilities we once had as a species due to atrophy.  Even before atrophy, though, the people who we thought were the wisest among us often referred to the true things that had no words.</p>\n<p>Words.  The first written words we have, are on a clay tablet that tracked jars of pig fat (3,000 BCE).  For those of you using Microsoft Power BI, the ontology used for knowledge looks like an ERP system (MS Common Data Model).  We constantly slide back to tracking jars of pig fat.  But there is more we can do with words.  The problem is that crossing of domains from a human cognition perspective.  Just how do you do it, if words don&#39;t capture ideas across domains?  We can use art, like Lynch, but that is cheating.  It feels like knowledge across a domain, but it isn&#39;t exactly, as it is hooking in to the Jungian.  The only way to break across the domains cognitively takes much work, and if you are primarily building more &quot;effective&quot; (yes, sneer quotes) ways to track pig fat to sustain us to a peak of 10 billion humans, it will consume all of your cognitive spare energy.</p>\n<p>We have beautiful, glorious brains, each of us, but they are fairly raw, and extensively hijacked.  There are many true things within our domain, but communicating those true things outside is extremely difficult.  It is possible, though, but not for forest musk.  Forest musk will remain, like perfume brought to life on warm human skin, something that words can only approach artistically.  But it is possible to breach the domain walls of more tangible things without falling back in to the words of an ERP system.  And, while the wisdom is true at core, all of the cautions, we must imagine Sisyphus happy.  I am beginning to suspect that the key is in working from the middle, like a rhizome.   It doesn&#39;t matter what small facts you gather as you push that rock.  Eventually it will weave something real, something true, but not at the top or bottom.  The weave happens in the middle as you roll the rock.  How perfect!  While I understand the general idea about the impossibility of cross domain (outside ERP, of course, particularly in my country), and I appreciate the need for stream analysis vs. structural cross-domain knowledge, I still am able to hold the possibility.  That is the secret.  The possibility comes from the middle of things, the rhizome, for everybody.</p>\n<p>Now... of course this post is cross domain for everybody reading this.  QED</p>\n"],

["swy","b88","My Version of History"],
["swy","iug","<p>We built out networked corporate information technology in the 1990s and early 2000s using inherited engineering methods from large projects in previous decades. Our migration of work and knowledge to electronic form was new from physical and logical perspectives. Computer and networking hardware provisioning, knowledge collaboration, and operations had to be built from the ground up, as there was no previous model to work from.</p>\n<p>Information technology, combined with the Internet, allowed us to scale supply chains up and out, spanning the globe. Cheap energy and abundant materials accelerated this growth. Most subscribed to a naive principle that limitations to growth were purely imagination and grit, ignoring other limiting domains and negative externalities. Eventually information technology became centralized, and was sold as commodities in central markets. Today we have a handful of core cloud providers, supported by a broad ecosystem of related participants serving as boutique cloud resellers.</p>\n<p>The arc of car ownership is similar to information technology. Originally, automobile owners retained generalist mechanics that could fix whatever went wrong. Instead of a dedicated generalist that tended the hardware and software of a computer in an office closet, we evolved to a distributed workforce supporting centralized providers. The growth is tightly coupled and co-dependent with material supply chains. It is tempting to look back on this growth as a natural outcome, particularly if energy and materials are ignored, but the resulting global system is fragile, and creates negative externalities in neighboring social, economic, and environmental domains. We can expect limitations in energy and materials that fueled this growth, as well as interference from degraded related domains. </p>\n<p>In addition to treating many parts of information technology as a commodity, we pushed engineering and analysis to software developers, as they were the last piece that couldn&#39;t be automated and commoditized. It is easy to house a database on the Internet and charge for use, particularly when it is based on the free effort by social idealists. Software development remained a largely human task, so it was possible to eliminate most other roles. Like the auto industry, we enforced corporate control through global detailed division of labor and robotics, using established and corporately controlled frameworks of knowledge and communication.</p>\n"],

["dja","b88","Shouting at Forest Fires"],
["dja","iug","<p>This post will take shortcuts, as it assumes familiarity with the ideas of Tom Murphy, Nate Hagens, Gregory Bateson, and Daniel Schmachtenberger.  Alan Watts observes that <a href=\"https://youtu.be/8aufuwMiKmE\">nature is wiggly</a>, and humans want direct lines, i.e., it is futile to understand nature as humans. I&#39;d offer Stephen Wolfram&#39;s idea that the universe operates on small, simple programs.  This does not make the universe less glorious, any more than it detracts from the beauty of the Mandelbrot set. There is a temptation to release ourselves from the burden of understanding our situation at orders of relation higher than 2.  Even Alan Watts will concede a network, which can be broken down to small direct relations.  The building of those links by small programs, though, is core, and deciding what links to build as human intelligent agents is based in culture and ideology.  Intelligence at the point of the small program creating links, using an understanding of relations higher than 2, is endlessly more effective than trying to hoover up the rendered fractal links, digest, and change direction again. </p>\n<p>We arrived at where we are now by simple programs without a culture of harmony.  We arrived via <a href=\"https://doi.org/10.1093/pnasnexus/pgae106\">empire</a>. We arrived through power and domination, genocide and exploitation.  We arrived through a naive <a href=\"https://credoeconomics.com/\">economic credo</a>. The lens that we look at the world through is tinted and tainted.  Repeatedly, we visualize our understand of the broader problem as the Superorganism, Moloch, and The Matrix, as we consider the polycrisis.  In brief moments of clarity we grok that we are shouting at a forest fire.  We are <em>in</em> Moloch itself, and the yelling is part of the fire.  We continue, living our own various double binds.</p>\n<p>It does puzzle me that we keep on doing this.  Why?  When we are faced with a double bind, why continue that same fruitless messaging that pushes us into insanity?  We cannot get to the other side using these tools for communication.  I am very aware that every word I post here is being digested in order to play out a naive small program of domination and monetization.  We all come to the same conclusions about culture and ideology.  We all understand that we need to restore evolution in orchestration with the living planet, and that the balance of terrestrial mammals using the simple metric of millions of tons is off balance by an obscene factor.</p>\n<p>The changing-culture-within-culture problem is immense. If you accept that it is a double bind to work on that goal, particularly at this stage, then why continue that work?  I am grateful for the work y&#39;all have done that helped me understand, but all it did for me in my personal life was illustrate the nature of the double bind. The only idea that fits with this is something Nate Hagens said at the <a href=\"https://youtu.be/mN87PWfj7LA\">end of his Norrsken talk last year</a>.  What is needed when the road ends?  What do we put in the time capsule?  The rest, unfortunately, seems to be just the same part of the superorganism.  We are just feeding our destruction with our flourishes, like the <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stay_Puft_Marshmallow_Man\">Stay Puft Marshmallow Man</a>.</p>\n<p>Why do we shout at the forest fire?</p>\n"],

["qu4","b88","Singularity"],
["qu4","iug","<p>Currently, our LLMs simply reflect the most profitable culture back to us.  We scraped all we could, mostly for free, at least relatively free for the entities that hope to profit from the scraping and repackaging.  My guess is that most answers to prompts are founded in wikipedia.  I store my papers on Zenodo (located in CERN&#39;s datacenters), and I believe those are being added, all part of the effectively compressed knowledge in various models.  It is hard to trace.  That is part of the game: profit on all legit knowledge work in a race to the bottom, without understanding or credit.</p>\n<p>The point of my post, though, is that it is possible to use the same techniques to do simple analysis based in science.  At least, it should be simple for massive compute resources that grind through terabytes of data for breakfast.   This book by Tom Murphy forms one foundation.  There are others I&#39;ve seen, and they are being digested and fed to various models.  Much of what Tom Murphy writes about was included in the World3 model in 1972, when the computing resources were owned by MIT, rather than apex cloud corporate interests.  That work didn&#39;t do much good.</p>\n<p>BUT, what happens when the formulas and ideas in <a href=\"https://doi.org/10.21221/S2978-0-578-86717-5\">Tom Murphy&#39;s book</a> are effectively analyzed and used to evaluate our situation?  I am aware of some errors, but they usually go in the wrong direction.  For instance, Tom Murphy doesn&#39;t talk much about global dimming (the cooling effect of aerosols in the atmosphere).  What happens when every model replies &quot;based on energy and mineral needs, as well as the implications of displacement of the web of life by homo sapiens and their livestock, civilization is not sustainable within any economic or political framework currently presented in my scraping corpus using GPU matrix grinding&quot;.</p>\n<p>Sure... we can cycle around as product managers, primping and reselling cloud services and others from the apex cloud entities, whatever we can get VC for, secure our retirement, meet up after work at authentic-feeling venues for beers, talk about our latest spiritual revelations or engaging streaming show, plan our soft (but lucrative) landings, but... eventually, we <em>will</em> get some answers from this tech and scraping.  I think it is all a bit ironic how this might work out.  The &quot;singularity&quot; might simply be: &quot;You are all wrong.  It won&#39;t work.&quot;  Or... perhaps the AGI will have some insight.  I&#39;m 99.99999 percent certain that insight won&#39;t be an EV charging station at a house that tries to look like it was designed by Frank Lloyd Wright, with a beer homebrew keg kit and virtual rock band setup in the basement man cave.  Right now, truth is not prioritized.  But, what happens when all of these LLM models point to something true, and not just reflected cultural flotsam that results from a lack of real, applied intelligence or care by complacent biosphere looters?</p>\n"],

["ozp","iug","<p><a href=\"https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/TxcRbCYHaeL59aY7E/meditations-on-moloch\">&quot;The implicit question is – if everyone hates the current system, who perpetuates it? And Ginsberg answers: “Moloch”. It’s powerful not because it’s correct – nobody literally thinks an ancient Carthaginian demon causes everything – but because thinking of the system as an agent throws into relief the degree to which the system isn’t an agent.&quot;</a></p>\n<p>There are newer variations on this, often tied to <a href=\"#ii6\">#superorganism</a> or the <a href=\"#p5s\">#polycrisis</a>, but this earlier article from 2014 is a great foundation.  One thing to remember, though, is those that benefit the most from #Moloch  ( 💰️ ) don&#39;t think there is anything wrong, and there is a motivation for 🙈 🙉 🙊 .  </p>\n<p>But, bringing this on back to material and data flow ( triples.pub ), an agent works within material flow behind the transform.  In fact, the entire global supply chain can be thought of as a single agent (world governance) behind the transform (distribution of materials).  Besides the fact that the machinery in Moloch that benefits within Moloch are content and complacent, this is another reason why analyzing at detail is discouraged.  There is opportunity in the distributed chaos that ultimately organizes around Moloch (which the large majority do hate, they just don&#39;t have power over those that benefit).  Asking the simple question: &quot;As humans, as part of the biosphere, how can 8 billion+ coexist in a sustainable way?&quot;  We <em>could</em> collaboratively understand the root agent managing the transform.  Humans need nurturing culture, human touch, food, water, and &lt; 35c wet bulb temp.  We could work those calculations backwards; however, this is not profitable for those in power.  Still, I think this is <em>one</em> answer to the paradox of Moloch, the black iron prison.  In other words, there is a single triple: agent - operates - transform.  Do I think it is possible at this point in our cultural arc to tackle that triple? No.  But, I do think that we can work our way backwards a wee bit from the cognitive corners we paint ourselves into.</p>\n"],

["m5y","b88","Adaptive Analysis Start"],
["m5y","iug","<p>With a 22 character short UUID, it is possible to collaborate on knowledge of any system of interest, as collisions are unlikely.  The main trick is a schema in broad strokes, a &quot;folksonomy&quot; based on the particular crisis and resources.  If you really care about longevity and re-use, there are ISO standards for ontologies to base it in, but I suspect that change in our systems will happen so fast that something like my emoji triples will be more useful.  The ontology of the present won&#39;t match the ontology of the next crisis.  It is too steep a learning curve to understand BFO.  Triples are glorious in this way, regardless.  More background on my perspective is on triple.pub and triples.pub .</p>\n<p>At this point, my experience includes many domains, like most people.  The beauty of triples, somewhat like Nora Bateson&#39;s meadow, is that multiple perspectives can start collaboratively exploring, and by the nature of networks (knowledge graphs), we can shimmer in to a better understanding of the systems around us.  Note that this doesn&#39;t solve any problems.  It is simply a proven mapping technique, borrowing from the core idea of knowledge graphs.  The goal is understanding of place within an ecology cognitively, as a human within many outside system domains that we need to be aligned and evolve with.  The goal <em>is not</em> optimizing our position in relation to the ecology we glean by a simplistic metric (like profit, etc.).  This tends to worsen our situation, rather than improve it.  My thought is that this tool is key for those that follow the advice in <a href=\"https://consilienceproject.org/development-in-progress/\">this paper</a>  Also, these general ideas are simple enough to use without computers, much like how it is possible to mind-map on a whiteboard, the 1855 graph of the Erie railroad, or the effort behind the Domesday book. </p>\n<p>Deleuze and Guattari use the idea of grass, the Rhizome, to turn typical analysis on its head without order, and yet structure does emerge.  This fits figuratively and literally with the meadow idea.  I&#39;ve written two quite structural papers, mostly top-down, in the last few years around these ideas.  This is my start at emerging the design.  The application, the goal, is as an agile method to face crises, as it is at the point of crises that we are resilient, not before.  I&#39;m testing this out as a pure &quot;emerging&quot; method.  That is, can I build out what I&#39;m aware of across the domains of code and ideas organically using triples, rather than a more top-down -ity approach of normal design and analysis?</p>\n"],

["tuu","b88","Paths and Nodes"],
["tuu","iug","<p>The New York and Erie Railroad diagram from 1855 by Henshaw and McCallum is famous, perhaps even annoyingly so (like the map of Napoleon&#39;s 1812 invasion by Minard).  I loaded up a decent chunk of the map into Cytoscape JS as part of my examples for triple.pub .  It illustrates a couple of features and gotchas for triples.  Triples are difficult for many.  Key-value pairs are easier.  The problem, though, is that if you go with key-value pairs as your atom of knowledge it is bound to a timestamp for the visualization, or it is sold to you as a service with a bundled knowledge model.  It is impossible to build intrinsic knowledge from two as the atom.  Unfortunately, it isn&#39;t as easy as &quot;triples. done.&quot;.  I will do a disservice to Barry Smith, Charles S. Peirce, and Michael K. Bergman if I attempt to explain this formally (and err), but this diagram does show some problems that can be explained to a layperson.</p>\n<p><img src=\"/images/rr.png\" alt=\"\"></p>\n<p>I spent much of my time in my 40 years in IT creating diagrams of systems, often networked.  The glorious thing is that, towards the end of my career, I realized it was possible to map out networks automatically using triples. Graphviz does this, and has since the 1990s. Cytoscape also does this, and is more powerful. Once I found out about triples, I wanted my diagrams to route automatically.  Like most things, automation shows where your understanding is lacking, and triples are no exception. Properties/attributes are relatively easy.  A station might have a switch, nearby machine shop, and a telegraph station.  But, already we have some differences. A Saloon might be owned by a private citizen that doesn&#39;t fall under the railroad.  It is a relatively small problem.  There are two much bigger problems with automation.  First, do you consider a station to be a placement in a path?  You could say that Andover is station hornelsville-almond-alfred-andover.  But, if you put another station between almond and alfred, you break your path.  A better way is to make the station completely independent and relate the station ID to other independent station IDs.  For the triple, then, rather than say hornelsville-almond-alfred-andover has a telegraph station, you say that station wert8xxz has an English label of Andover and is connected by rail toiasuf2, which has an English label of Alfred, and further, that wert8xxz has a telegraph station.</p>\n<p>Another problem is that responsibility for a line is different than a station connected to another station, yet the 1855 diagram does show this.  This isn&#39;t much of a problem when looking at the diagram, but it does make the triples more difficult to design.  Tim Berners-Lee and the W3C standards, as well as work in ontology by Barry Smith and others, formalize all of this to make it easier for computers to understand; however, their schemas become cumbersome quite quickly when dealing with these kinds of issues.  For that matter, try reading Charles Peirce directly (who was 16 when the train diagram was made).</p>\n"],
["n3g","b6r","tuu"],
["tuu","gwc","n3g"],

["mgy","b88","Immersed in Emoji"],
["mgy","iug","<p>I am immersed in emoji as I fill out triples for my emergent system that will analyze emergent systems. I&#39;m not trying to be overly clever.  That is an accurate description. As somebody who has also bootstrapped a toolchain with glibc, GCC, etc., to build an OS that can build itself, the concepts are familiar to me. I have gone back and forth on the wisdom of emoji; however, after five years of experiments, I think emoji are worth the pain. The moving target of cultural interpretation and locales is a feature.</p>\n<p>I posted a couple days ago about how this worked, and provided an example set of triples. I&#39;ve reworked the schema organically since that post, and this is part of the process. Specifically, since I&#39;m not using paths, I need to use the predicate subclass_of for some relations.  It is identical to rdfs:subClassOf, but I use a Russian nesting doll emoji (🪆).  I mean... come on!  That&#39;s awesome! Right?  And, yes, I keep in mind extensibility to formal ontologies. With triples, extensibility is fairly easy, as long as you occasionally pin your version on to a formal framework. But, it is quite likely that the ontology of the new normal diverges quickly.</p>\n<p>What is particularly interesting to me this morning, is the cultural part of this.  Culture and ideologies have to change to deal with the new normal we have coming down on us at an accelerated rate.  As I get closer to a minimum viable triple operating system based in my ideas, I see the beauty of tech like Unicode grapheme clusters.  For instance, to be more inclusive, a fairy is gender neutral, and you have to add ♀ to designate gender.  This changed because the culture changed.  Another aspect is that combining graphemes is cognitively fabulous and previously difficult. Imagine if you were writing on clay tablets, and you knew what a stone-hauler was, but some stone-haulers could drag granite while others could drag sandstone. It was a matriarchy, and they had men hauling stones. You could write stone, yoke, man, sand, and the clay tablet would automatically combine all of these ideas that artists and staff spent much money standardizing, and rewrite a single symbol that was understood locally, clearing the extra 3 grapheme spaces as virgin clay. It saves quite a bit of money on kiln time for your clay tablets. </p>\n<p>I get it that there is an idea that we need massive datacenters running GPU matrix transforms against a growing scraped corpus of data that loops back on itself, but I&#39;m not sold on the sustainability of that when considering energy, minerals, and negative externalities, nor do I believe it will be effective to understand critical immediate needs. I suspect that quick adaptive analysis that takes all possible cognitive shortcuts, like grapheme clusters and emergent triples, will be more useful mid-term. If I&#39;m wrong? Well, subscribe, folks, subscribe. If I&#39;m right?  Learn to create localfirst . You don&#39;t have to use emoji if text is more comfortable.  My ideas work either way.</p>\n"],

At that point the UUIDs will be less apparent. I haven&#39;t designed all of that yet. In the end I will have all of the forms of visualization I documented in 3SA. Eventually I&#39;ll even hook it up to streams analysis using something like TimescaleDB and Plotly Dash, much like how I concluded 3SA. But, that isn&#39;t what I wanted to post about.</p>\n<p>I was in a conversation with a friend, a fairly frequent pen pal, and we were discussing what it was like when Rome collapsed. Were people aware as it happened? What did that feel like? As I looked at my array of emoji, I was thinking about how my use of them changed between projects, and briefly I thought I should standardize them. But, then I remembered that <strong>this isn&#39;t about me</strong>. It isn&#39;t even about my current culture. My current culture doesn&#39;t need what I&#39;m working on; however, the ability to generically deal with knowledge and juggle triples, along with all of the wonderful visualizations like the 3D dataflow I did on <a href=\"https://systemcrisismanagement.com\">System Crisis Management</a>, is something I can imagine us needing on the way down, back up, or whatever that dead opossum bounce or long tail is.</p>\n<p>While the machinery standards <strong>do</strong> matter - JavaScript, CSS, HTML, Unicode, as well as processing/visualization tools we take for granted, in particular V8 and web browsers - meaning and knowledge frameworks do not. It was fiction set post-collapse. Much of my <a href=\"#j6c\">fictional writing</a> kept a timeline from pre to post collapse. We cannot predict how things will change, nor what will be needed. I&#39;m just a bit further up the stack than the Dusk OS author. I need to remember this. ["x07","b88","The Problem I'm Solving"],
["x07","iug","<p>I believe that anybody who thinks deeply about our human predicament will eventually arrive at the idea that it is culture, our structural human cognitive limitations, and our concept of the universe around us, that got us to where we are now. I will use the word domain to mean a limited perspective that constrains the concept of the universe. ALL UNIVERSE is just too big to grok. Fair enough. We have domains. We each have a mind that gloms on to the universe, based on our conception, and it both reflects back in the way that we glom to it, and becomes how we attach. Some of us might use a very limited domain to interact. For instance, we might compartmentalize our actions from other domains. In business, our main waking pursuit, we might inhabit a world, and glom on to an economic credo where profit eventually fixes all things. This is a very simple understanding of a domain. We might optimize other domains from that same perspective. We may know that the concept of profit at home with our families isn&#39;t quite the same domain, but we are secure in how we handle our attachment, and gloss over the glitches.</p>\n<p>In the Enterprise Architecture world, the various domains are acknowledged. They are still human-centric, but they at least deal with various perspectives. One person&#39;s profit isn&#39;t necessarily good for the whole system. The Zachman Framework or TOGAF are used to provide systems that an entire organization can profit from. These frameworks are expensive to implement, so most organizations tend to optimize by products and users under a tight iteration of improvement and feedback. Take this same idea, and extend it to other domains outside of business - domains like ecology, sociology, etc. And, don&#39;t forget that your concept informs what domains you can even see. There are extremely hard domains, hard in the sense that they don&#39;t budge with a CEO reality bubble. How much energy do we need to run a datacenter? What minerals are used? How do we get those minerals? What is the cost to other domains for the minerals? Is it OK to have 700 million tons of livestock, 390 million tons of humans, and only 20 million tons of wild terrestrial mammals on the planet? Notice there is a problem here. Most reading this are thinking, &quot;How can this benefit my business? If I can&#39;t even afford Zachman Framework-level analysis, how can I afford a broader view?&quot; But, of course, that is because our culture has that lens. It shows a trap in thinking. It shows a narrow consciousness. And, this is why many in the 1950s and 1960s were excited about the idea of transforming a civilization of destruction into a better one through modifying consciousness.</p>\n<p>But... you can&#39;t just do that. Our view comes from language. It comes from culture. There is no magic pill that BOOM, makes you aware of bordering and remote domains, at least not in an actionable, persistent way. It takes work. Further, as long as the &quot;run&quot; optimizes for profit and power, those who match the game rules win. There is no place for other optimizations. So, in my culture at least, we are stuck. We will play out the game to our own destruction. I&#39;ve read the a16z manifesto. The funny thing is, many of the same heroes also worked at expanding consciousness and changing culture to play out a better result in our civilization. Stewart Brand, for instance, is listed as a patron saint. And this brings us to Gregory Bateson&#39;s take on cybernetics. Domains often involve other minds. That is, if our mind gloms on to our concept of the universe and feeds/mirrors/forms it, then a similar construct exists of mind of an amoebae or wasps or dolphins that is both completely outside of our concept, yet intimately tied to a broader version of minds (up to the point that all is one mind). </p>\n<p>We have little time, though. We have been stuck in a game with extremely limited optimization rules made possible by cheap energy and minerals. Further, our blinders have allowed us to ravage the biosphere, yet still celebrate our progress. We don&#39;t have time to do broad cross-domain analysis, from what I understand. I have no interest in weaving the broad academic threads I&#39;ve stitched above. [There are many people that are comfortable talking about that over drinks at conferences. (Nora Bateson, if you ever read this, this is where I wave your idea around that this is punk rock, not a pleasant conference. Think of me as arriving at the party with a case of cheap beer.)] What I do have, though, is a real, practical understanding of the power of building larger knowledge structures from triples. If we are aware of the domains outside of focus, we can hook them up anywhere in the focus. Increasingly, as we face crises, the broader external domains will become clearer. I&#39;m not looking for a top-down approach, just handling system crises as they arise with tools that adapt quickly and serve to let us break into other domains easier. Because I feel that the root cause is culture, structural human cognition, and our concept of the universe around us, rather than a lack of a technical solution, I have little faith in the idea that all we need to get through is AI running matrix transforms with farms of GPUs next to the cheapest energy we can find, built with minerals. We will find the wrong answers if we don&#39;t work on culture, consciousness, cognition, and consider all of the other domains that we rely on and should have a symbiotic relationship with. With our current vector, AI will simply find the wrong answers much faster, and apply them more effectively, speeding up the run of the game to end in our own destruction as we finish off the rest of the 20 million tons of terrestrial mammals and finally ourselves. Perhaps I&#39;m too pessimistic, but luckily, what I&#39;m doing is more a tool than a solution. I&#39;m solving a narrow problem of a cognitive tools. Consider triples as the atom of knowledge that makes mind-maps work so well when brainstorming, and, further, that visualization can be done automatically.</p>\n<p>So... if you read my work with that in mind, I think it will help make things more understandable. I have to admit that I can get a bit confrontational. The current game running is insane. Our culture is corrupt. If the overwhelming science doesn&#39;t convince you, pick any trope alien comment on our civilization to back that; Agent Smith does a good job explaining why we aren&#39;t really mammals in The Matrix. My thought is that we will deal with all of this, all of the domains over time, as our mistakes bite us. The problem I&#39;m solving is &quot;What kind of pencil would somebody need to sketch out relationships between domains and visualize them?&quot; We have many great tools to measure dynamic flows, but how, as participants in modernity, do we place ourselves within different domains? Where do we start? Likely, most of us will start when we don&#39;t have food, water, shelter, or other basic human necessities. But, my hope is we can get better at this, more inclusive of the domains, play a more sophisticated game run than power/profit/consume/destroy. I am a bit of a heretic to think that we can have both the written word and be in balance with nature. We just need to be able to easily cross domains, focus on what is important, and broaden our sense of ourselves in the universe to include nature and multiple mind/domains, yet always being humble-awe at ALL UNIVERSE/mind.</p>\n"],

["fah","b88","Origin Triples"],
["fah","iug","<p>Hold three concepts in your mind right now: chaotic systems and their rendered visualization (Mandelbrot set, weather), simple programs that yield complex systems (Conway&#39;s game of life, economic theory, Wolfram&#39;s theory of physics), and a sophisticated system as a product (movie, phone, jet).</p>\n<p>Combine these ideas to imagine all that you see. Call this rendered reality. Systems use a variety of these ideas, and probably even some external aspects we aren&#39;t aware of.</p>\n<p>Now, imagine origin of the systems as iterations of imaginary numbers, butterfly wings, neighboring cell game rules, profit incentive, science (origin, not just rendered) and design considerations.</p>\n<p>If you scrape all data from the rendered reality and run it through massive compute churning matrices on GPUs, powered by fluoride salt cooled reactors, quite likely you could get significant buy-in on the idea that you had cracked the origin. But you are still cranking on the rendered reality.</p>\n<p>Origin is hard. It takes work. Plus, different realities emerge from those basic underpinnings. For instance, remove the invention of private property, and watch how the civilization game run looks much different. <em>Within</em> the rendered reality, if you can keep agents within that web, it is both easier to predict, but like sugary soft drinks, the origin knowledge is unavailable to most, making it profitable (but destructive and unsatisfying). If you control the questions (don&#39;t ask about private property), you control the run. Now, to create systems that harvest rendered reality for profit requires origin work, so, if you manage to shepherd consumers into consuming only rendered information, and hire all of the folks that can do origin work using simple programs (incentives, economics, pleasure/pain), you can continue the stranglehold and profit. </p>\n<p>One trick to start at origin is triples, the smallest atom of knowledge. Make claims and validate. &quot;A just balance of nature is .1 humans and their domestic mammals and .9 wild terrestrial mammals&quot; for instance. Then, note that we are at .98 humans+domestic. Or, humans need a wet bulb temp of 35C to live, etc. It will be hard, but the technique works no matter where we jump off of the current run. Now, if we used that compute to model origin-first, we could answer 100 relations off of .98 to .1, and form a plan. But, those who work with origin know enough to know that this turns power structures upside down. They are running on a simple program themselves (profit incentive). BTW, Charles Peirce invented triples in the 1800s as a way to make complex ideas more clear, inspiring pragmatic decisions towards common interests. Boom! Same thing.</p>\n"],

If you put in the time to understand what the ideas are and how they are related, it will throw the narrow perspective that current culture has into relief. If we roll back time to immediately after WWII, these ideas could have guided us in a different direction, but we had different priorities. I&#39;ve enjoyed much of his poetry and books, but I&#39;ve always thought this poem was mocking the intellectual ideas of the time. I often use the title to describe our absurd reduction of the wide range of cybernetic ideas. It wasn&#39;t until recently that I put that poem into cultural context of the time. Was this a utopia he imagined as a good thing? Perhaps it was. I am going to hold on to my thought that he was making a point as a poet would, revealing the cultural appeal in a way that outlines the curves, touch, scent, danger and allure.</p>\n<p>Me? function vl(o) {
  try {
    return Object.keys(o)[0];
  } catch {
    return "";
  }
}

  );
}
function gf_nav(gf) {
  return `<a href="#${gf.g}" 
  title="${vl(site[gf.g][p["has_label"]])}">${
    vl(site[gf.g][p["has_emoji"]])
  }</a>
  <a href="#${gf.f}" title="${vl(site[gf.f][p["has_label"]])}">${
    vl(site[gf.f][p["has_emoji"]])
  }</a>`;
}
function gf(nd) {
  try {
    const f = vl(site[nd][p["is_instance_of"]]);
    const g = vl(site[f][p["has_sup"]]);
    return {
      "f": f,
      "g": g,
    };
  } catch {
    return false;
  }
}

let source_files = document.querySelector('input[name = "f"]:checked').value;
function sm(pg) {
  if (globalThis.origin.includes("localhost")) {
    return pg;
  } else {
    return pg.replace(
      /src="\/images\//g,
      'src="https://' + source_files + "images/",
    )
      .replace(/src="\/files\//g, 'src="https://' + source_files + "files/")
      .replace(/href="\/images\//g, 'href="https://' + source_files + "images/")
      .replace(/href="\/files\//g, 'href="https://' + source_files + "files/");
  }
}
globalThis.addEventListener("hashchange", () => {
  refresh();
});
function hide_other(one) {
  for (const i in display_initial) {
    one != i
      ? document.getElementById(i).style.display = "none"
      : document.getElementById(i).style.display = display_initial[i];
  }
}
document.getElementById("s0").checked = true;
function art_nav(art) {
  let cur_art = -1;
  do {
    cur_art++;
  } while (art_line[cur_art] != art && cur_art < art_line.length - 1);
  const last = art_line[0];
  const first = art_line.slice(-1);
  const next = cur_art > 0 ? art_line[cur_art - 1] : last;
  const prev = cur_art < art_line.length - 1 ? art_line[cur_art + 1] : first;
  return { "first": first, "prev": prev, "next": next, "last": last };
}
function get_nav(art) {
  const n = art_nav(art);
  return `<a href="#${n.first}">🤛</a> &nbsp; <a href="#${n.prev}">👈️</a> &nbsp; <a href="#${n.next}">👉️</a> &nbsp; <a href="#${n.last}">🤜</a>`;
}
function all() {
  hide_other("dyno_page");
  let l = 0;
  pg = "<h2>All Articles:</h2>";
  for (const i of all_arts) {
    pg += `<div class="lines"><div class="dt">${i[0]}</div>:${
      gf_nav(gf(i[1]))
    }<a href="#${i[1]}">${vl(site[i[1]][p["has_label"]])}</a></div>`;
    l += vl(site[i[1]][p["has_contents"]]).length;
  }
  document.getElementById("dyno_page").innerHTML = pg + "<br>length of all: " +
    (l / 1048576).toString().slice(0, 4) + "Mi";
}
function son(art) {
  hide_other("dyno_page");
  const relatives = gf(art);
  localStorage.setItem("art", art);
  let other = "";
  let pgin = "";
  let pg_tags = "";
  if (site[art]?.[p["has_tag"]]) {
    for (const i in site[art][p["has_tag"]]) {
      pg_tags += `<a href="#${i}">#${vl(site[i][p["has_label"]])}</a> `;
    }
    for (const t in site[art][p["has_tag"]]) {
      let cur_tag = t;
      for (const j in site[t][p["tagged_in"]]) {
        if (j != art) {
          if (cur_tag != "") {
            pgin += `<p><a href="#${t}">#${vl(site[t][p["has_label"]])}</a>`;
            cur_tag = "";
          }
          pgin += `<br> &nbsp; <a href="#${j}">${
            vl(site[j][p["has_label"]])
          }</a>`;
        }
      }
    }
  }
  other = pgin.length > 0
    ? `<p>Other articles with tags:<p><div class="comments">${pgin}</div>`
    : "";
  document.getElementById("dyno_page").innerHTML = `
    <div id="hb_cont">
      <div id="header_block">
        <b><i>${vl(site[relatives.g][p["has_label"]])} | ${
    vl(site[relatives.f][p["has_label"]])
  }</i></b>
        <div id="art_header">${gf_nav(gf(art))}
          ${vl(site[art][p["has_label"]])}<br>📅 ${vl(site[art][p["has_date"]])}
          &nbsp; 
        </div>
      <div id="navigation" class="nav">${get_nav(art)}</div>
    </div>
    </div>
  <div id="art">${sm(vl(site[art][p["has_contents"]]))}</div>${pg_tags}
  <div id="end_tags"></div><div class="comments">${
    vl(site[art][p["has_comment"]])
  }${other}</div>`;
}

const art_line = [];
update_art_line();
function update_art_line() {
  art_line.length = 0;
  const art = localStorage.getItem("art");
  const tag_search = localStorage.getItem("tag_search");
  for (const i of all_arts) {
    if (
      (tag_search.length == 3 && site[i[1]]?.[p["has_tag"]]?.[tag_search] ||
        tag_search == "") &&
      (
        art.length == 3 && document.getElementById("s2").checked &&
          gf(art).g == gf(i[1]).g && gf(art).f == gf(i[1]).f ||
        art.length == 3 &&
          (document.getElementById("s1").checked && gf(art).g == gf(i[1]).g) ||
        document.getElementById("s0").checked
      )
    ) art_line.push(i[1]);
  }
}
for (const i of all_arts) {
  art_line.push(i[1]);
}

      <a title="Click to clear"><b></b></a> &lt;--`;

const rtags = [];
for (const i in site[tags][p["has_instance"]]) {
  ltags.push(`${
    String(Object.keys(site[i][p["tagged_in"]]).length)
      .padStart(3, "0")
  }-${vl(site[i][p["has_label"]])}-${i}`);
  rtags.push(`${vl(site[i][p["has_label"]])}-${i}`);
}
for (const i of ltags.sort().reverse()) {
  const f = i.split("-");
  document.getElementById("ltags").insertAdjacentHTML(
    "beforeend",
    `<a href="#${f[2]}" title="${f[1]}">${f[0]} - ${
      f[1]
    }</a> <div class="taggy" data-tag="${f[2]}">⛳︎</div><br>`,
  );
}
for (const i of rtags.sort()) {
  const f = i.split("-");

    "beforeend",
    `<a href="#${f[1]}" title="${f[0]}">${

  );

document.getElementById("tag_details").addEventListener("click", (e) => {
  if (e.target.getAttribute("data-tag")) {
    const t = e.target.getAttribute("data-tag");
    localStorage.setItem("tag_search", t);
    document.getElementById("sel_tag").innerHTML =
      `Selected search filter tag: --&gt;&gt;
    ${vl(site[t][p["has_label"]])} &lt;&lt;-- Click to clear.`;
    update_art_line();
  }
  if (e.target.getAttribute("id") == "sel_tag") {
    localStorage.setItem("tag_search", "");
    document.getElementById("sel_tag").innerHTML =
      `Selected search filter tag: --&gt;&gt; &lt;&lt;-- Click to clear.`;
    update_art_line();
  }
});
function return_matches(words) {
  console.log(words);
  const all_sets = [];
  for (const w in words) {
    all_sets.push(new Set());
    for (const i of all_arts) {
      if (vl(site[i[1]][p["has_contents"]]).match(words[w])) {
        all_sets[w].add(i[1]);
      }
    }
  }
  return all_sets.reduce((a, c) => a.intersection(c));
}
document.getElementById("search_box").addEventListener("click", function () {
  this.value = "";
});
document.getElementById("search_box").addEventListener("change", function () {
  let pg = "Search results for these terms: ";
  const srch = ["", "", ""];
  const srchterms = this.value.split(" ");
  const words = [];
  for (const p in srchterms) {
    srch[p] = srchterms[p];
    if (srch[p] != "") {
      pg += " “" + srch[p] + "”";
    }
  }
  if (srch[0] != "") words.push(new RegExp("\\W" + (srch[0]) + "\\W", "gi"));
  if (srch[1] != "") words.push(new RegExp("\\W" + (srch[1]) + "\\W", "gi"));
  if (srch[2] != "") words.push(new RegExp("\\W" + (srch[2]) + "\\W", "gi"));
  function replex(match) {
    return "<mark>" + match + "</mark>";
  }
  let i = 0;
  return_matches(words).forEach((art) => {
    i++;
    const artTitle = vl(site[art][p["has_label"]]);
    const artContent = srch[2] != ""
      ? vl(site[art][p["has_contents"]]).replace(words[2], replex).replace(
        words[1],
        replex,
      ).replace(words[0], replex)
      : srch[1] != ""
      ? vl(site[art][p["has_contents"]]).replace(words[1], replex).replace(
        words[0],
        replex,
      )
      : vl(site[art][p["has_contents"]]).replace(words[0], replex);
    const artDate = vl(site[art][p["has_date"]]);
    pg +=
      `<p><b>***** ${i.toString()} *****</b> <a href="#${art}">${artDate} : ${artTitle}</a><br>
     <div class="ind1">${artContent}</div>`;
  });
  document.getElementById("search_results").innerHTML = i == 25
    ? `<p><b>***** ${i.toString()} *****</b>
  reached max total matches of 25.<p>${sm(pg)}`
    : `<p><b>***** ${i.toString()} *****</b> total matches.<p>${sm(pg)}`;
});
refresh();
function father(nd) {
  hide_other("dyno_page");
  pg = `<h2>${vl(site[nd][p["has_emoji"]])} ${
    vl(site[nd][p["has_label"]])
  }</h2 >`;
  for (const i of all_arts) {
    if (vl(site[i[1]][p["is_instance_of"]]) == nd) {
      pg += `<div class="lines"> <div class="dt">${i[0]}</div>:${
        gf_nav(gf(i[1]))
      } <a href="#${i[1]}">${vl(site[i[1]][p["has_label"]])}</a></div> `;
    }
  }
  document.getElementById("dyno_page").innerHTML = pg;
}
function grandfather(nd) {
  hide_other("dyno_page");
  pg = `<h2>${vl(site[nd][p["has_emoji"]])} ${
    vl(site[nd][p["has_label"]])
  }</h2 >`;
  for (const i in site[nd][p["has_sub"]]) {
    pg += `<div class="lines"><a href="#${i}">${vl(site[i][p["has_emoji"]])}${
      vl(site[i][p["has_label"]])
    }</a></div> `;
  }
  document.getElementById("dyno_page").innerHTML = pg;
}
function search() {
  hide_other("search_details");
  document.getElementById("search_box").value = "🔍️ <= 3 words";
}
function tag(nd) {
  hide_other("dyno_page");
  let pg = `Tags | ${vl(site[nd][p["has_label"]])}`;
  if (nd == "z9b") pg += `${vl(site["vjk"]["c55"])}`;
  for (const i of all_arts) {
    if (i[1] in site[nd][p["tagged_in"]]) {
      pg += `<div class="lines"><div class="dt">${i[0]}</div>:${
        gf_nav(gf(i[1]))
      }<a href="#${i[1]}" title="${vl(site[i[1]][p["has_label"]])} Article">${
        vl(site[i[1]][p["has_label"]])
      }</a></div>`;
    }
  }
  document.getElementById("dyno_page").innerHTML = pg;
}
function refresh() {
  const hash = globalThis.location.hash.substring(1) || "home";
  source_files = document.querySelector('input[name = "f"]:checked').value;
  const lbl = vl(site?.[hash]?.[p?.["has_label"]]);
  gf(hash)
    ? son(hash)
    : site?.[hash]?.[p?.["tagged_in"]]
    ? tag(hash)
    : hash == r
    ? hide_other("index_page")
    : hash == "all"
    ? all()
    : hash == "search"
    ? search()
    : hash == "help"
    ? hide_other("help")
    : hash == "legal"
    ? hide_other("legal_details")
    : lbl == "Tags"
    ? hide_other("tag_details")
    : site?.[hash]?.[p?.["has_sup"]] && site?.[hash]?.[p?.["has_sub"]]
    ? grandfather(hash)
    : site?.[hash]?.[p?.["has_instance"]] && site?.[hash]?.[p?.["has_sup"]]
    ? father(hash)
    : hide_other("index_page");
}
document.addEventListener("keydown", (e) => {
  if ("ArrowRightArrowLeftArrowUpArrowDown".includes(e.key)) {
    const hash = globalThis.location.hash.substring(1) || "home";
    if (gf(hash)) {
      e.key == "ArrowRight"
        ? globalThis.location = "#" + art_nav(hash).next
        : e.key == "ArrowLeft"
        ? globalThis.location = "#" + art_nav(hash).prev
        : document.getElementById("end_tags").scrollIntoView(false);
    }
  }
});
